text: The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure of the colon to access the superior pole of the kidney and adrenal gland. An ultrasonic shear is used for bloodless dissection, revealing the spleen and tail of the pancreas. The patient is positioned laterally, allowing gravity to aid in the mobilization of these organs away from the adrenal gland. A peritoneal incision is extended to the left diaphragm, with the tumor visible at the bottom of the screen. The dissection continues until the upper short gastric vessels are visible, indicating that the spleen, pancreas, and stomach have been successfully mobilized away from the surgical field.

The surgeon clips and divides a small arterial twig entering the lower pole of the tumor from the renal artery. Using a blunt instrument, the tumor is retracted upwards to identify and dissect the middle adrenal vein. Another arterial twig next to the venous structure is clipped and divided. A small gauze piece is placed in the abdomen to control any oozing. The middle adrenal vein is isolated by teasing away fatty tissue with a bipolar device, clipped multiple times, and then divided.
The surgeon carefully identifies and divides a small arterial twig located just behind the vein, ensuring there is no bleeding from the area during the procedure.

The surgeon separates the adrenal mass from the surface of the kidney, revealing small blood vessels entering the tumor. Each vessel is clipped and divided using a harmonic scalpel. The dissection continues towards the diaphragm, with careful coagulation of tissue strands along the superior surface of the kidney. A small vessel entering the tumor is controlled with clips, and the superior adrenal artery from the inferior phrenic is clipped and divided. The tumor is now attached only by small tissue to the diaphragm and left crus, which is finally separated. Hemostasis is checked.

The gauze piece is placed inside the surgical field, and the adrenal tumor, measuring nine by eight centimeters, is placed inside a plastic retrieval bag. The tumor is then removed through a fan and steel incision, which is visually smaller and more comfortable than a large upper abdominal transverse incision.
